bibliographic.citation: A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse, supposed to have been written by Thomas Brampton, S.T.P., in the year 1414; together with a Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard, in Latin and in English Verse, with notes by William Henry Black (London: printed for the Percy Society by T. Richards, 1842).
bibliographic.title: A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse, supposed to have been written by Thomas Brampton, S.T.P., in the year 1414; together with a Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard, in Latin and in English Verse

entities.description: William Henry Black's Percy Society edition of the Sloane-based main paraphrase, collated against a Harley fragment, together with two manuscript-derived appendices and a separate notes apparatus.
entities.label: Black, A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms (1842)
notes: CME00017 encodes printed pp. 1-54 only; it excludes the preface, contents, and notes on pp. 55-70.
The blank printed p. 48 is represented by a page-break marker before Appendix I.

rights.basis: U-M presents the edition-derived selection as public-domain material under No Copyright-United States; the edition was published in 1842.
rights.component: The historical text and editorial material selected in CME00017

entities.description: A composite manuscript whose paper section produced in the 1460s contains a fragment of the A version on ff. 13r-17v, corresponding to stanzas 62-116. Black collated it against Sloane MS 1853 and judged it later, inaccurate, corrupt, and linguistically modernized.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 1704

notes: The relevant fragment is only a small part of a composite whose other sections range from the fourteenth to seventeenth centuries.

entities.description: A composite parchment codex whose first-quarter-fifteenth-century English and Latin prayer section on ff. 71r-97v includes the Middle English Saint Bernard legend and eight-verse psalter transcribed by Black from ff. 86v-88v for Appendix II.
entities.label: British Library, Royal MS 17 A XXVII
notes: The 1842 scan reads XXVII. The XML's XXVIII reading is incompatible with the current catalog description of Royal MS 17 A XXVIII as a late-sixteenth-century 25-folio album.
The earlier part belongs to the thirteenth-century Katherine Group; the Appendix II source lies in the distinct fifteenth-century section.

next_steps: Inspect the attribution hands in complete images of Sloane MS 1853 and Trinity R.3.20.
Review post-Sutherland scholarship on Brampton's identity, the six-witness stemma, and A/B version chronology.
Keep author, annotator, and composition-date claims separate unless stronger evidence emerges.
openQuestions.question: Who composed the main paraphrase, and what weight should be given to the later Brampton attributions dated 1413 and 1414?

next_steps: Obtain complete color images and a current curatorial assessment of support, script, decoration, reingrossed portions, and the attribution hand.
Consult modern codicological and palaeographical scholarship before assigning a single witness date.
openQuestions.question: How should Sloane MS 1853's physical date and textual layers be reconciled across Black's early-fifteenth-century description, later scholarship's fifteenth-century dating, and the current British Library catalog's sixteenth-century date?

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether historical complete imaging of Royal MS 17 A XXVII and Harley MS 1704 can be restored or supplied.
Request complete-object imaging for Sloane MS 1853, Harley MSS 1704 and 1845, and Royal MS 17 A XXVII, including covers, endleaves, blanks, and adjacent works.
Ask Cambridge University Library about existing full imaging or imaging of Ff.2.38.
Ask the Pepys Library about complete photography of MSS 1584 and 2030 and secure written reuse terms before caching supplied files.
notes: The Trinity R.3.20 bundle is complete; the PICRYL Sloane crop is explicitly partial.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, reusable digital facsimiles be obtained for the seven physical manuscripts lacking a currently cached complete public surrogate?

next_steps: Ask U-M Library for item-level keying specifications, source-copy identification, proofreading history, and correction logs.
Propose correcting the Appendix II source headnote to Royal MS 17 A XXVII in the upstream CME encoding while preserving the documented source reading history.
openQuestions.question: What item-specific production record identifies U-M's exact keying copy and explains its selection of pp. 1-54, omission of the apparatus, and XXVIII shelfmark error?

next_steps: Compare dialect, meter, vocabulary, and transmission against all main-poem witnesses and other versions of the Saint Bernard eight-verses legend.
Retain separate work-part modeling unless evidence supports a closer relationship.
openQuestions.question: Is Appendix II's Middle English Legendary Psalter authorially or textually related to the main paraphrase beyond Black's editorial juxtaposition?

M. R. James, Bibliotheca Pepysiana: Mediaeval Manuscripts (1923)
M. R. James, Bibliotheca Pepysiana: Mediaeval Manuscripts (1923)

reviewNotes: The cataloged unit is Black's three-part edited selection on pp. 1-54, not one organic medieval work, the complete 1842 volume, or any one manuscript.
The main poem's six witnesses are recorded as physical witnesses of A and B textual versions; Black directly transcribed Sloane MS 1853 and collated Harley MS 1704 only.
Black's 1414 Sloane attribution and Trinity R.3.20's 1413 attribution are later annotations and are not converted into a secure composition date.
The 1842 scan reads Royal MS 17 A XXVII for Appendix II. CME00017's XXVIII headnote conflicts with both the scan and current British Library catalog descriptions and is treated as an encoding error.
Witnesses, printed edition, page-image surrogates, digital encoding, repository artifact, and scholarly descriptions are modeled as distinct layers.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact copies University of Michigan CME item CME00017, an edition-level encoding of printed pp. 1-54 of William Henry Black's 1842 Percy Society volume. The selection contains a 124-stanza Middle English paraphrase based on British Library Sloane MS 1853 and collated with Harley MS 1704, Latin Appendix I from Harley MS 1845, and Middle English Appendix II from Royal MS 17 A XXVII. It excludes the preface, contents, and Black's printed notes on pp. 55-70. Six witnesses of the main paraphrase are recorded. A complete scan of the 1842 edition, a complete 406-canvas IIIF bundle for Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.20, two complete scholarly support sources, and one partial Sloane f. 3 crop are cached. No complete public facsimile was found for Sloane MS 1853, Harley MS 1704, Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38, Pepys MSS 1584 or 2030, Harley MS 1845, or a currently working public delivery of Royal MS 17 A XXVII.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous medieval authors; the main paraphrase is conventionally attributed to Thomas Brampton
catalogSummary.date: Early fifteenth century for the main paraphrase; later annotations give 1413 and 1414; edition published 1842
catalogSummary.region: England; no single more precise composition place is established for all three parts
catalogSummary.title: A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse, together with a Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard

catalogingSubject.label: A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse, together with a Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection encoded from William Henry Black's 1842 Percy Society volume: the 124-stanza Middle English penitential-psalm paraphrase on printed pp. 1-47, the blank p. 48, Latin Appendix I on pp. 49-51, and Middle English Appendix II on pp. 51-54. The selection excludes the volume's preface, contents, and printed notes on pp. 55-70. It is neither one manuscript nor one securely unified medieval work.

contentParts.label: Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms
notes: The first six stanzas form the introduction; stanza 7 begins the first psalm.
The work is the A textual version in modern scholarship, represented by Black's Sloane-based edition.
contentParts.summary: A 124-stanza Middle English penitential and devotional poem: six introductory stanzas followed by translation, paraphrase, and commentary on the seven penitential psalms, generally keyed to Latin Vulgate verses and repeatedly using the antiphon 'Ne reminiscaris, Domine'.

contentParts.label: Appendix I: Latin Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard
contentParts.summary: A Latin account of Saint Bernard compelling the Devil to reveal eight efficacious psalm verses, followed by the verses and two prayers.

contentParts.label: Appendix II: Middle English Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard
notes: The XML headnote says Royal MS 17 A XXVIII. The 1842 scan reads XXVII, and current British Library descriptions show that Royal MS 17 A XXVII contains the relevant fifteenth-century material while Royal MS 17 A XXVIII is an incompatible late-sixteenth-century album.
Black explicitly regarded common authorship with the main paraphrase as uncertain.
contentParts.summary: A Middle English prose account of the Saint Bernard legend followed by eight Middle English eight-line devotional stanzas, each introduced by a Latin psalm-verse incipit.

assertion.notes: A broad scholarly composition range, kept distinct from the dates written by later annotators.
This is a traditional date in a later, partly damaged annotation, not a contemporary colophon.

dateStatements.display: 1414 in the later attribution recorded from Sloane MS 1853

assertion.notes: The one-year discrepancy is preserved rather than normalized to Black's 1414 title-page claim.

dateStatements.display: 1413 in the later attribution in Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.20

extent.description: Three top-level units: the main 124-stanza poem (992 verse lines), Latin prose Appendix I, and mixed prose-and-verse Appendix II with eight stanzas (64 verse lines). The XML has 132 LG elements, 1,056 L elements, 14 P elements, 54 PB elements, 137 HEAD elements, and seven NOTE elements. Paragraph counts are encoding structure, not a defensible item inventory.

formStatements.description: The selection combines two verse-centered Middle English parts with a Latin prose legend, quoted psalm verses, and prayers.
A 124-stanza poem in eight-line stanzas, with Latin prose epigraphs.
A Latin prose legend framing eight quoted psalm verses and two prose prayers.
A Middle English prose legend followed by eight eight-line stanzas with Latin epigraphs.

scope.description: Two prayers following the eight psalm verses
The complete text of Appendix I and embedded antiphon, psalm verses, headings, rubrics, and epigraphs in the verse parts
Editorial headings, source notes, and footnotes retained on printed pp. 1-54

notes: Work metadata describes the U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection; witness holdings, facsimile completeness, access, and image rights remain in the linked lineage manifest.
The traditional Thomas Brampton attribution is scoped only to the main paraphrase and marked disputed.

Royal MS 17 A XXVII is used as Appendix II's source identity because the 1842 scan and current catalog evidence agree; the XML's XXVIII reading is documented as an encoding error, not silently propagated.
The absence of Black's pp. 55-70 notes matters: the encoding retains selected editorial interventions but not the complete printed apparatus that explains them.

next_steps: Inspect the attribution hands in complete images of Sloane MS 1853 and Trinity R.3.20.
Review post-Sutherland scholarship on Brampton's identity, the six-witness stemma, and the relative dates of the A and B versions.
Keep author, annotator, and composition-date claims separate unless stronger evidence emerges.
openQuestions.question: Who composed the main paraphrase, and what weight should be given to the later Brampton attributions dated 1413 and 1414?

next_steps: Obtain complete color images and current curatorial assessment of support, script, decoration, and added attribution hand.
Consult modern codicological and palaeographical scholarship before assigning a single witness date.
openQuestions.question: How should the physical date and textual layers of Sloane MS 1853 be reconciled across Black's early-fifteenth-century description, later scholarship's fifteenth-century dating, and the current British Library catalog's sixteenth-century date?

next_steps: Compare dialect, meter, vocabulary, and transmission across Royal MS 17 A XXVII and witnesses of the main poem.
Review scholarship on the Saint Bernard eight-verses legend and its Middle English versions.
openQuestions.question: Is the Middle English Legendary Psalter in Appendix II related authorially or textually to the main penitential-psalm paraphrase beyond Black's juxtaposition?

assertion.notes: The broad English context is supported; no single locality is assigned to all three parts.
No author is securely established for the complete edited selection.

scope.description: The three medieval textual units collectively, without implying a single author

assertion.notes: Black accepted the attribution, and modern scholarship retains it conventionally, but the naming evidence occurs only in later annotations in Sloane MS 1853 and Trinity R.3.20.

scope.description: The manuscript-derived text on printed pp. 1-54
The main Sloane text collated against the Harley fragment

subjects.label: Legend of Saint Bernard and the Devil

summaries.value: An edition-level selection centered on a 124-stanza Middle English paraphrase and meditation on the Seven Penitential Psalms, conventionally but uncertainly attributed to Thomas Brampton. Black's two appendices add Latin and Middle English versions of a Saint Bernard legend concerning eight protective psalm verses, with prayers and an eight-stanza English rendering.

The main poem begins with six penitential introductory stanzas and then paraphrases the seven psalms in stanzas 7-124. Appendix I gives a Latin legend, eight Latin psalm verses, and two prayers from Harley MS 1845. Appendix II gives an English legend and eight eight-line Middle English stanzas from Royal MS 17 A XXVII.

CME00017 encodes printed pp. 1-54 of the 1842 volume, including the intentionally blank p. 48, but not the preface, contents, or Black's notes on pp. 55-70. Those omitted notes contain the fuller apparatus for Harley variants, supplied text, and editorial intervention.

The selection is cataloged at edition level because its main paraphrase and two appendices have different immediate manuscript sources and are not securely works by one author. The manuscript witnesses, Black's editorial layer, U-M encoding, and repository artifact remain distinct in lineage.

assertion.notes: A supplied catalog title combining the two title-page content clauses represented in the encoded selection.
